lei: noun. A garland of flowers in Hawaii.
leu: noun. The unit of currency of Romania, equal to one hundred bani.
ley: noun. Arable land used temporarily for hay or grazing.
lie: verb. To rest in a horizontal position on a surface.
lin: verb. To desist, to stop, to cease.
line: noun. A path through two or more points (compare ‘segment’); a continuous mark, including as made by a pen; any path, curved or straight.
liney: adjective. Resembling or characterised by lines.
lune: noun. A fit of lunacy or madness; a period of frenzy; a crazy or unreasonable freak.
lye: noun. An alkaline liquid made by leaching ashes (usually wood ashes).
ulu: noun. An all-purpose knife traditionally used by Yup'ik, Inuit, and Aleut women.
unique: adjective. Being the only one of its kind; unequaled, unparalleled or unmatched.
yen: noun. The unit of Japanese currency (symbol: ¥) since 1871, divided into 100 sen.
yin: noun. A principle in Chinese and related East Asian philosophies associated with dark, cool, female, etc. elements of the natural world.
yule: noun. Alternative letter-case form of Yule.
eli: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Nding.
eyn: noun. Alternative form of eyen: plural of eye.
lue: verb. To sift using a sieve, particularly in mining tin or silver.
nul: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Nusa Laut.
nuque: noun. The nape of the neck.
nye: noun. A brood or flock of pheasants.
que: noun. The name of the Latin script letter Q / q.
qui: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Quileute.
quine: noun. A program that produces its own source code as output.
ule: noun. A Mexican and Central American tree (Castilla elastica), related to the breadfruit tree, whose milky juice contains caoutchouc.
yue: noun. An ancient Chinese unit of volume, notionally equivalent to the space occupied by 1200 millet seeds.
